---
title: "Factors Influencing Stroke — A Multi-Method Analysis"
subtitle: "Supervised Learning, Unsupervised Learning, Causal Inference, and Ensemble Modelling"
author: "Portfolio 2026"
date: today
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    embed-resources: true
    fig-width: 10
    fig-height: 6
jupyter: python3
execute:
  warning: false
---

## Introduction

Stroke is a leading cause of death and long-term disability worldwide. This report
applies **four complementary analytical approaches** to identify the combination of
patient features most associated with stroke risk, using the
[Kaggle Healthcare Stroke Dataset](https://www.kaggle.com/asaumya/healthcare-dataset-stroke-data)
(43,400 observations, 11 features).

| Analysis | Approach | Goal |
|----------|----------|------|
| I | Supervised learning | Identify feature combinations predictive of stroke |
| II | Unsupervised learning | Discover patient sub-populations and their stroke susceptibility |
| III | Causal inference | Estimate causal effects of modifiable risk factors |
| IV | Ensemble model | Combine methods into a unified feature importance ranking |

```{python}
#| label: setup
import warnings
from pathlib import Path

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import shap
from scipy import stats
from sklearn.calibration import calibration_curve
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier, RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.metrics import (
    PrecisionRecallDisplay,
    RocCurveDisplay,
    average_precision_score,
    precision_recall_curve,
    precision_recall_fscore_support,
    roc_auc_score,
    silhouette_score,
)
from sklearn.model_selection import (
    StratifiedKFold,
    cross_val_predict,
    cross_val_score,
)
from sklearn.preprocessing import OneHotEncoder, StandardScaler

warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", category=FutureWarning)
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", category=UserWarning)

OUTDIR = Path("output")
OUTDIR.mkdir(exist_ok=True)


def savefig(fig, name):
    """Save figure to output directory at 300 DPI."""
    fig.savefig(OUTDIR / name, dpi=300, bbox_inches="tight")
```

---

## 1. Exploratory Data Analysis

### 1.1 Data Overview

```{python}
#| label: load-data
df_raw = pd.read_csv("Data/train_2v.csv")
print(f"Dataset: {df_raw.shape[0]:,} rows × {df_raw.shape[1]} columns")
df_raw.head()
```

```{python}
#| label: data-types
print("Column types and non-null counts:\n")
df_raw.info()
```

```{python}
#| label: descriptive-stats
df_raw.describe(include="all").T
```

### 1.2 Missing Data

```{python}
#| label: fig-missing
#| fig-cap: "Missing value pattern. BMI has 3.4% missing values; smoking_status has 30.6% blank entries corresponding predominantly to children."
smoking_blank = (df_raw["smoking_status"].isna() | (df_raw["smoking_status"] == "")).sum()
bmi_null = df_raw["bmi"].isna().sum()

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(8, 7))

axes[0].barh(["smoking_status", "bmi"], [smoking_blank, bmi_null],
             color=["#E64B35", "#4DBBD5"])
for i, v in enumerate([smoking_blank, bmi_null]):
    axes[0].text(v + 100, i, f"{v:,} ({v/len(df_raw):.1%})", va="center")
axes[0].set_xlabel("Missing Count")
axes[0].set_title("Missing Values by Column")

mask_blank = df_raw["smoking_status"].isna() | (df_raw["smoking_status"] == "")
axes[1].hist(df_raw.loc[mask_blank, "age"], bins=30, color="#E64B35", alpha=0.7,
             label="Smoking blank", density=True)
axes[1].hist(df_raw.loc[~mask_blank, "age"], bins=30, color="#4DBBD5", alpha=0.5,
             label="Smoking known", density=True)
axes[1].set_xlabel("Age")
axes[1].set_ylabel("Density")
axes[1].set_title("Age Distribution: Missing vs Known Smoking Status")
axes[1].legend()

fig.tight_layout()
savefig(fig, "01_missing_data.png")
plt.show()
```

The blank smoking entries are concentrated among patients under ~17, confirming this
is **informative missingness** (children without smoking history), not random.

### 1.3 Target Variable — Class Imbalance

```{python}
#| label: fig-class-balance
#| fig-cap: "Severe class imbalance: only 1.8% of observations experienced a stroke."
stroke_counts = df_raw["stroke"].value_counts().sort_index()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4))
bars = ax.bar(["No Stroke (0)", "Stroke (1)"], stroke_counts.values,
              color=["#4DBBD5", "#E64B35"], edgecolor="white")
for bar, v in zip(bars, stroke_counts.values):
    ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2, v + 200,
            f"{v:,}\n({v/len(df_raw):.1%})", ha="center", fontsize=11)
ax.set_ylabel("Count")
ax.set_title("Stroke Class Distribution")
fig.tight_layout()
savefig(fig, "02_class_balance.png")
plt.show()
```

### 1.4 Pre-processing

```{python}
#| label: preprocess
df = df_raw.copy()

# Drop id (no predictive value)
df = df.drop(columns=["id"])

# Drop 11 "Other" gender rows (too few to model)
print(f"Gender value counts:\n{df['gender'].value_counts()}\n")
df = df[df["gender"] != "Other"].copy()

# BMI: age-stratified median imputation
df["age_decade"] = (df["age"] // 10) * 10
bmi_medians = df.groupby("age_decade")["bmi"].transform("median")
df["bmi"] = df["bmi"].fillna(bmi_medians)
df = df.drop(columns=["age_decade"])

# Smoking status: encode blanks as "unknown"
df["smoking_status"] = df["smoking_status"].replace("", "unknown").fillna("unknown")

print(f"After cleaning: {df.shape[0]:,} rows, {df.shape[1]} columns")
print(f"Remaining nulls: {df.isnull().sum().sum()}")
```

### 1.5 Univariate Feature Distributions

```{python}
#| label: fig-numeric-distributions
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of numeric features stratified by stroke status. Stroke patients tend to be older and have higher glucose levels."
numeric_cols = ["age", "avg_glucose_level", "bmi"]
fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(8, 10))

for ax, col in zip(axes, numeric_cols):
    for label, color, name in [(0, "#4DBBD5", "No Stroke"), (1, "#E64B35", "Stroke")]:
        sub = df.loc[df["stroke"] == label, col]
        ax.hist(sub, bins=30, alpha=0.6, color=color, label=name, density=True)
    ax.set_xlabel(col.replace("_", " ").title())
    ax.set_ylabel("Density")
    ax.legend(fontsize=8)

fig.suptitle("Numeric Feature Distributions by Stroke Status", fontsize=13)
fig.tight_layout()
savefig(fig, "03_numeric_distributions.png")
plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-categorical-distributions
#| fig-cap: "Stroke prevalence across categorical feature levels. Hypertension, heart disease, older age (via work type proxy), and formerly smoked show elevated stroke rates."
cat_cols = ["gender", "hypertension", "heart_disease", "ever_married",
            "work_type", "Residence_type", "smoking_status"]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(len(cat_cols), 1, figsize=(8, len(cat_cols) * 2.5))

for ax, col in zip(axes, cat_cols):
    ct = df.groupby(col)["stroke"].agg(["sum", "count"])
    ct["rate"] = ct["sum"] / ct["count"]
    ct = ct.sort_values("rate", ascending=True)
    colors = ["#E64B35" if r > df["stroke"].mean() else "#4DBBD5" for r in ct["rate"]]
    ax.barh(ct.index.astype(str), ct["rate"], color=colors)
    ax.set_xlabel("Stroke Rate")
    ax.set_title(col)
    ax.axvline(df["stroke"].mean(), color="black", linestyle="--",
               linewidth=0.8, alpha=0.5)

fig.suptitle("Stroke Prevalence by Categorical Feature", fontsize=13)
fig.tight_layout()
savefig(fig, "04_categorical_distributions.png")
plt.show()
```

### 1.6 Correlation Analysis

```{python}
#| label: fig-correlation
#| fig-cap: "Correlation matrix of numeric and binary features. Age shows the strongest linear association with stroke."
df_corr = df.copy()
df_corr["gender"] = (df_corr["gender"] == "Male").astype(int)
df_corr["ever_married"] = (df_corr["ever_married"] == "Yes").astype(int)
df_corr["Residence_type"] = (df_corr["Residence_type"] == "Urban").astype(int)

corr_cols = ["age", "avg_glucose_level", "bmi", "hypertension",
             "heart_disease", "gender", "ever_married", "Residence_type", "stroke"]
corr = df_corr[corr_cols].corr()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 7))
mask = np.triu(np.ones_like(corr, dtype=bool), k=1)
sns.heatmap(corr, mask=mask, annot=True, fmt=".2f", cmap="RdBu_r",
            center=0, vmin=-1, vmax=1, square=True, ax=ax)
ax.set_title("Feature Correlation Matrix")
fig.tight_layout()
savefig(fig, "05_correlation_matrix.png")
plt.show()
```

### 1.7 Key EDA Findings

- **Age** is the strongest univariate predictor (r ≈ 0.25 with stroke).
- **Hypertension** and **heart disease** have elevated stroke rates (~4-5× baseline).
- **Average glucose level** shows a right-skewed distribution with higher values among stroke patients.
- **BMI** has a weak association with stroke.
- **Smoking**: "formerly smoked" has the highest stroke rate, while "unknown" (children) has the lowest.
- Class imbalance is severe (1.8%) — standard accuracy metrics will be misleading.

---

## 2. Feature Engineering

```{python}
#| label: feature-engineering
target = "stroke"
y = df[target]

numeric_features = ["age", "avg_glucose_level", "bmi"]
categorical_features = ["gender", "hypertension", "heart_disease",
                        "ever_married", "work_type", "Residence_type",
                        "smoking_status"]

preprocessor = ColumnTransformer(
    transformers=[
        ("num", StandardScaler(), numeric_features),
        ("cat", OneHotEncoder(drop="if_binary", sparse_output=False,
                              handle_unknown="infrequent_if_exist"),
         categorical_features),
    ]
)

X = preprocessor.fit_transform(df[numeric_features + categorical_features])
feature_names = (numeric_features +
                 preprocessor.named_transformers_["cat"]
                 .get_feature_names_out(categorical_features).tolist())
print(f"Feature matrix: {X.shape[0]:,} samples × {X.shape[1]} features")
print(f"Features: {feature_names}")
```

---

## 3. Analysis I — Supervised Statistical Learning

### 3.1 Handling Class Imbalance — Why Not SMOTE?

The dataset has a 1.8% positive rate. Traditional approaches rely on **SMOTE**
(Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique) to generate synthetic minority samples.
However, recent evidence shows SMOTE is problematic:

1. **Noisy synthetic samples**: SMOTE interpolates linearly between nearest
   neighbours without domain semantics, producing physiologically implausible
   patient profiles (e.g., blending a child's BMI with an elderly patient's
   comorbidities).
2. **Data leakage risk**: even within pipelines, synthetic samples can inflate
   evaluation metrics without improving real-world performance.
3. **Unnecessary with strong learners**: modern tree-based models handle natural
   class distributions well when combined with cost-sensitive learning.

Instead, we use the **modern best-practice approach**:

- **Cost-sensitive learning**: `class_weight="balanced"` (logistic regression,
  random forest) or `scale_pos_weight` (gradient boosting) adjusts the loss
  function to upweight minority samples without fabricating data.
- **Threshold tuning**: train on the natural distribution and optimise the
  decision threshold post-hoc using the precision-recall curve.
- **Proper metrics**: PR-AUC as the primary metric (more informative than ROC-AUC
  for rare events), supplemented by ROC-AUC and calibrated F1.

References: [Train in Data (2025)](https://www.blog.trainindata.com/should-you-use-imbalanced-learn-in-2025/),
[imbalanced-learn common pitfalls](https://imbalanced-learn.org/stable/common_pitfalls.html),
[Springer cost-sensitive review (2023)](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10462-023-10652-8).

```{python}
#| label: imbalance-comparison
cv = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)

# Compare baseline vs cost-sensitive approaches on the natural distribution
strategies = {
    "Baseline LR (no correction)": LogisticRegression(
        max_iter=1000, random_state=42),
    "Cost-sensitive LR (balanced)": LogisticRegression(
        max_iter=1000, class_weight="balanced", random_state=42),
    "Cost-sensitive RF (balanced)": RandomForestClassifier(
        n_estimators=200, max_depth=10, class_weight="balanced",
        random_state=42, n_jobs=-1),
    "Cost-sensitive GB (scale_pos_weight)": GradientBoostingClassifier(
        n_estimators=200, max_depth=5, random_state=42,
        # Manual sample_weight applied via fit — approximate here with init settings
    ),
}

# We need to handle GB scale_pos_weight differently since sklearn GB doesn't
# have it built-in. We'll use sample_weight in cross_val_predict instead.
n_pos = y.sum()
n_neg = len(y) - n_pos
scale_weight = n_neg / n_pos

results_imbalance = []
for name, model in strategies.items():
    if "GB" in name:
        # For GB, use sample_weight via manual CV
        aucs = []
        pr_aucs = []
        for train_idx, val_idx in cv.split(X, y):
            X_tr, X_val = X[train_idx], X[val_idx]
            y_tr, y_val = y.iloc[train_idx], y.iloc[val_idx]
            sw = np.where(y_tr == 1, scale_weight, 1.0)
            model.fit(X_tr, y_tr, sample_weight=sw)
            probs = model.predict_proba(X_val)[:, 1]
            aucs.append(roc_auc_score(y_val, probs))
            pr_aucs.append(average_precision_score(y_val, probs))
        results_imbalance.append({
            "Strategy": name,
            "ROC-AUC": f"{np.mean(aucs):.4f}",
            "PR-AUC": f"{np.mean(pr_aucs):.4f}",
        })
    else:
        y_prob = cross_val_predict(model, X, y, cv=cv, method="predict_proba")[:, 1]
        results_imbalance.append({
            "Strategy": name,
            "ROC-AUC": f"{roc_auc_score(y, y_prob):.4f}",
            "PR-AUC": f"{average_precision_score(y, y_prob):.4f}",
        })

pd.DataFrame(results_imbalance)
```

Cost-sensitive learning substantially improves both ROC-AUC and PR-AUC over the
naive baseline, confirming that addressing imbalance at the algorithm level is
effective — without fabricating synthetic data.

### 3.2 Model Comparison

```{python}
#| label: model-training
models = {
    "Logistic Regression": LogisticRegression(
        max_iter=1000, class_weight="balanced", random_state=42),
    "Random Forest": RandomForestClassifier(
        n_estimators=200, max_depth=10, class_weight="balanced",
        random_state=42, n_jobs=-1),
}

model_probs = {}
model_results = []
for name, model in models.items():
    probs = cross_val_predict(model, X, y, cv=cv, method="predict_proba")[:, 1]
    model_probs[name] = probs
    auc = roc_auc_score(y, probs)
    pr_auc = average_precision_score(y, probs)
    scores_roc = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=cv, scoring="roc_auc")
    scores_pr = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=cv, scoring="average_precision")
    model_results.append({
        "Model": name,
        "ROC-AUC": f"{auc:.4f}",
        "ROC ± SD": f"{scores_roc.mean():.4f} ± {scores_roc.std():.4f}",
        "PR-AUC": f"{pr_auc:.4f}",
        "PR ± SD": f"{scores_pr.mean():.4f} ± {scores_pr.std():.4f}",
    })

# Gradient Boosting with sample_weight
gb_probs_all = np.zeros(len(y))
gb_roc_scores = []
gb_pr_scores = []
gb = GradientBoostingClassifier(n_estimators=200, max_depth=5, random_state=42)
for train_idx, val_idx in cv.split(X, y):
    X_tr, X_val = X[train_idx], X[val_idx]
    y_tr, y_val = y.iloc[train_idx], y.iloc[val_idx]
    sw = np.where(y_tr == 1, scale_weight, 1.0)
    gb.fit(X_tr, y_tr, sample_weight=sw)
    probs = gb.predict_proba(X_val)[:, 1]
    gb_probs_all[val_idx] = probs
    gb_roc_scores.append(roc_auc_score(y_val, probs))
    gb_pr_scores.append(average_precision_score(y_val, probs))

model_probs["Gradient Boosting"] = gb_probs_all
model_results.append({
    "Model": "Gradient Boosting",
    "ROC-AUC": f"{roc_auc_score(y, gb_probs_all):.4f}",
    "ROC ± SD": f"{np.mean(gb_roc_scores):.4f} ± {np.std(gb_roc_scores):.4f}",
    "PR-AUC": f"{average_precision_score(y, gb_probs_all):.4f}",
    "PR ± SD": f"{np.mean(gb_pr_scores):.4f} ± {np.std(gb_pr_scores):.4f}",
})

pd.DataFrame(model_results)
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-roc-pr
#| fig-cap: "Left: ROC curves. Right: Precision-Recall curves — the more informative metric for rare events. All models trained with cost-sensitive learning on the natural class distribution."
fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(8, 10))
colors = {"Logistic Regression": "#4DBBD5", "Random Forest": "#E64B35",
          "Gradient Boosting": "#00A087"}

for name, probs in model_probs.items():
    auc = roc_auc_score(y, probs)
    pr_auc = average_precision_score(y, probs)
    RocCurveDisplay.from_predictions(
        y, probs, name=f"{name} (AUC={auc:.3f})",
        ax=ax1, color=colors[name])
    PrecisionRecallDisplay.from_predictions(
        y, probs, name=f"{name} (AP={pr_auc:.3f})",
        ax=ax2, color=colors[name])

ax1.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], "k--", alpha=0.3, label="Chance")
ax1.set_title("ROC Curves — 5-Fold CV")
ax1.legend(loc="lower right", fontsize=8)

ax2.axhline(y.mean(), color="black", linestyle="--", alpha=0.3, label="Baseline prevalence")
ax2.set_title("Precision-Recall Curves — 5-Fold CV")
ax2.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8)

fig.tight_layout()
savefig(fig, "06_roc_pr_curves.png")
plt.show()
```

### 3.3 Threshold Tuning

Rather than using the default 0.5 threshold (arbitrary for imbalanced data), we
select the threshold that maximises F1 on the precision-recall curve.

```{python}
#| label: fig-threshold
#| fig-cap: "F1 score as a function of decision threshold for the Random Forest model. The optimal threshold is substantially below 0.5, reflecting the class imbalance."
best_model_name = "Random Forest"
probs_best = model_probs[best_model_name]

precision_vals, recall_vals, thresholds = precision_recall_curve(y, probs_best)
f1_scores = 2 * (precision_vals[:-1] * recall_vals[:-1]) / (
    precision_vals[:-1] + recall_vals[:-1] + 1e-10)
best_idx = np.argmax(f1_scores)
best_threshold = thresholds[best_idx]
best_f1 = f1_scores[best_idx]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
ax.plot(thresholds, f1_scores, color="#E64B35", linewidth=2)
ax.axvline(best_threshold, color="black", linestyle="--", alpha=0.5,
           label=f"Optimal threshold = {best_threshold:.3f}")
ax.axvline(0.5, color="gray", linestyle=":", alpha=0.5, label="Default threshold = 0.5")
ax.scatter([best_threshold], [best_f1], color="#E64B35", s=100, zorder=5)
ax.set_xlabel("Decision Threshold")
ax.set_ylabel("F1 Score")
ax.set_title(f"Threshold Optimisation — {best_model_name}")
ax.legend()
fig.tight_layout()
savefig(fig, "07_threshold_tuning.png")
plt.show()

print(f"Optimal threshold: {best_threshold:.3f}")
print(f"F1 at optimal threshold: {best_f1:.3f}")

# Performance at optimal vs default threshold
for thr, label in [(0.5, "Default (0.5)"), (best_threshold, "Optimised")]:
    preds = (probs_best >= thr).astype(int)
    p, r, f1, _ = precision_recall_fscore_support(y, preds, pos_label=1, average="binary")
    print(f"\n{label} threshold:")
    print(f"  Precision={p:.3f}  Recall={r:.3f}  F1={f1:.3f}")
```

### 3.4 Feature Importance — SHAP Values

```{python}
#| label: fig-shap
#| fig-cap: "SHAP beeswarm plot from the random forest model. Age and average glucose level dominate feature importance."
rf_final = RandomForestClassifier(
    n_estimators=200, max_depth=10, class_weight="balanced",
    random_state=42, n_jobs=-1)
rf_final.fit(X, y)

explainer = shap.TreeExplainer(rf_final)
shap_values = explainer.shap_values(X)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 7))
shap.summary_plot(shap_values[:, :, 1], X, feature_names=feature_names,
                  show=False, max_display=15)
plt.title("SHAP Feature Importance — Random Forest (Cost-Sensitive)",
          fontsize=13, pad=15)
plt.tight_layout()
savefig(plt.gcf(), "08_shap_importance.png")
plt.show()
```

### 3.5 Logistic Regression Coefficients

```{python}
#| label: fig-coef
#| fig-cap: "Logistic regression coefficients on standardised features. Positive values increase stroke risk."
lr_final = LogisticRegression(
    max_iter=1000, class_weight="balanced", random_state=42)
lr_final.fit(X, y)

coef_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Feature": feature_names,
    "Coefficient": lr_final.coef_[0],
}).sort_values("Coefficient")

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 7))
colors = ["#E64B35" if c > 0 else "#4DBBD5" for c in coef_df["Coefficient"]]
ax.barh(coef_df["Feature"], coef_df["Coefficient"], color=colors)
ax.axvline(0, color="black", linewidth=0.5)
ax.set_xlabel("Coefficient (log-odds)")
ax.set_title("Logistic Regression Coefficients (Standardised, Cost-Sensitive)")
plt.tight_layout()
savefig(fig, "09_lr_coefficients.png")
plt.show()
```

---

## 4. Analysis II — Unsupervised Statistical Learning

We use PCA for dimensionality reduction followed by K-Means clustering to identify
patient sub-populations, then examine their stroke susceptibility.

### 4.1 PCA — Dimensionality Reduction

```{python}
#| label: fig-pca-variance
#| fig-cap: "Cumulative explained variance by PCA components. The first 5 components capture ~60% of variance."
pca = PCA(random_state=42)
X_pca = pca.fit_transform(X)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4))
cumvar = np.cumsum(pca.explained_variance_ratio_)
ax.bar(range(1, len(cumvar)+1), pca.explained_variance_ratio_,
       color="#4DBBD5", alpha=0.7, label="Individual")
ax.plot(range(1, len(cumvar)+1), cumvar, "o-", color="#E64B35", label="Cumulative")
ax.axhline(0.8, color="black", linestyle="--", alpha=0.3)
ax.set_xlabel("Principal Component")
ax.set_ylabel("Explained Variance Ratio")
ax.set_title("PCA Explained Variance")
ax.legend()
fig.tight_layout()
savefig(fig, "10_pca_variance.png")
plt.show()
```

### 4.2 K-Means Clustering

```{python}
#| label: fig-silhouette
#| fig-cap: "Silhouette scores for different numbers of clusters."
X_pca5 = X_pca[:, :5]

sil_scores = []
K_range = range(2, 8)
for k in K_range:
    km = KMeans(n_clusters=k, random_state=42, n_init=10)
    labels = km.fit_predict(X_pca5)
    sil_scores.append(silhouette_score(X_pca5, labels))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
ax.plot(list(K_range), sil_scores, "o-", color="#E64B35")
ax.set_xlabel("Number of Clusters (K)")
ax.set_ylabel("Silhouette Score")
ax.set_title("Optimal Cluster Count")
fig.tight_layout()
savefig(fig, "11_silhouette_scores.png")
plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-clusters
#| fig-cap: "Patient clusters in PCA space (first two components), coloured by cluster assignment. Stroke cases (×) overlay the clusters."
best_k = list(K_range)[np.argmax(sil_scores)]
km_final = KMeans(n_clusters=best_k, random_state=42, n_init=10)
df["cluster"] = km_final.fit_predict(X_pca5)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))
cluster_colors = ["#E64B35", "#4DBBD5", "#00A087", "#F39B7F", "#8491B4"]
for c in range(best_k):
    mask = df["cluster"] == c
    ax.scatter(X_pca[mask, 0], X_pca[mask, 1], c=cluster_colors[c],
               alpha=0.15, s=5, label=f"Cluster {c}")
stroke_mask = df["stroke"] == 1
ax.scatter(X_pca[stroke_mask, 0], X_pca[stroke_mask, 1],
           c="black", marker="x", s=15, alpha=0.6, label="Stroke")
ax.set_xlabel("PC1")
ax.set_ylabel("PC2")
ax.set_title(f"K-Means Clusters (K={best_k}) in PCA Space")
ax.legend(markerscale=3)
fig.tight_layout()
savefig(fig, "12_clusters_pca.png")
plt.show()
```

### 4.3 Cluster Profiles and Stroke Susceptibility

```{python}
#| label: cluster-profiles
profile_cols = ["age", "avg_glucose_level", "bmi", "hypertension",
                "heart_disease", "stroke"]
cluster_profile = df.groupby("cluster")[profile_cols].mean().round(3)
cluster_profile["n"] = df.groupby("cluster").size()
cluster_profile["stroke_rate"] = (cluster_profile["stroke"] * 100).round(2).astype(str) + "%"
cluster_profile
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-cluster-stroke
#| fig-cap: "Stroke prevalence varies substantially across patient clusters, with the highest-risk cluster characterised by older age and elevated glucose."
fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(8, 10))

cluster_rates = df.groupby("cluster")["stroke"].mean() * 100
axes[0].bar([f"Cluster {c}" for c in range(best_k)], cluster_rates.values,
            color=cluster_colors[:best_k])
axes[0].set_ylabel("Stroke Rate (%)")
axes[0].set_title("Stroke Prevalence by Cluster")

cluster_age = df.groupby("cluster")["age"].mean()
axes[1].bar([f"Cluster {c}" for c in range(best_k)], cluster_age.values,
            color=cluster_colors[:best_k])
axes[1].set_ylabel("Mean Age")
axes[1].set_title("Mean Age by Cluster")

cluster_gluc = df.groupby("cluster")["avg_glucose_level"].mean()
axes[2].bar([f"Cluster {c}" for c in range(best_k)], cluster_gluc.values,
            color=cluster_colors[:best_k])
axes[2].set_ylabel("Mean Glucose")
axes[2].set_title("Mean Glucose by Cluster")

fig.tight_layout()
savefig(fig, "13_cluster_stroke_profiles.png")
plt.show()
```

---

## 5. Analysis III — Causal Inference

We use **Inverse Probability of Treatment Weighting (IPW)** to estimate the
Average Treatment Effect (ATE) of modifiable risk factors on stroke. IPW creates
a pseudo-population where the treatment is independent of measured confounders,
allowing a causal interpretation under the assumptions of no unmeasured confounding,
positivity, and consistency.

For each binary treatment (hypertension, heart disease, smoking), we:

1. Fit a propensity score model: P(treatment | confounders)
2. Compute IPW weights: 1/P(T|X) for treated, 1/(1−P(T|X)) for untreated
3. Estimate the weighted difference in stroke rates (ATE)
4. Bootstrap 95% confidence intervals

```{python}
#| label: causal-setup
df_causal = df.copy()
df_causal["gender_male"] = (df_causal["gender"] == "Male").astype(int)
df_causal["ever_married_yes"] = (df_causal["ever_married"] == "Yes").astype(int)
df_causal["urban"] = (df_causal["Residence_type"] == "Urban").astype(int)
df_causal["ever_smoked"] = df_causal["smoking_status"].isin(
    ["formerly smoked", "smokes"]).astype(int)

confounders = ["age", "avg_glucose_level", "bmi", "gender_male",
               "ever_married_yes", "urban"]


def ipw_ate(data, treatment_col, outcome_col, confounder_cols, n_bootstrap=500):
    """Estimate Average Treatment Effect using Inverse Probability Weighting.

    Args:
        data: DataFrame with treatment, outcome, and confounder columns.
        treatment_col: Name of the binary treatment column.
        outcome_col: Name of the binary outcome column.
        confounder_cols: List of confounder column names.
        n_bootstrap: Number of bootstrap iterations for CI.

    Returns:
        Dictionary with ATE, 95% CI lower and upper bounds.
    """
    def _ate_once(df_boot):
        X_c = df_boot[confounder_cols].values
        t = df_boot[treatment_col].values
        y_out = df_boot[outcome_col].values

        ps_model = LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, random_state=42)
        ps_model.fit(X_c, t)
        ps = ps_model.predict_proba(X_c)[:, 1]
        ps = np.clip(ps, 0.01, 0.99)

        w1 = t / ps
        w0 = (1 - t) / (1 - ps)

        ate = np.average(y_out, weights=w1) - np.average(y_out, weights=w0)
        return ate

    ate_point = _ate_once(data)

    rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
    boot_ates = []
    for _ in range(n_bootstrap):
        idx = rng.choice(len(data), size=len(data), replace=True)
        boot_ates.append(_ate_once(data.iloc[idx]))
    boot_ates = np.array(boot_ates)
    ci_low, ci_high = np.percentile(boot_ates, [2.5, 97.5])

    return {"ATE": ate_point, "CI_low": ci_low, "CI_high": ci_high}
```

```{python}
#| label: causal-estimates
treatments = {
    "Hypertension": "hypertension",
    "Heart Disease": "heart_disease",
    "Ever Smoked": "ever_smoked",
}

causal_results = []
for label, col in treatments.items():
    conf = [c for c in confounders if c != col]
    result = ipw_ate(df_causal, col, "stroke", conf)
    causal_results.append({
        "Treatment": label,
        "ATE (pp)": f"{result['ATE']*100:.2f}",
        "95% CI": f"[{result['CI_low']*100:.2f}, {result['CI_high']*100:.2f}]",
    })
    print(f"{label}: ATE = {result['ATE']*100:.2f} pp "
          f"[{result['CI_low']*100:.2f}, {result['CI_high']*100:.2f}]")

pd.DataFrame(causal_results)
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-causal
#| fig-cap: "IPW-estimated Average Treatment Effects (in percentage points) with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals. Hypertension and heart disease show the strongest causal effects on stroke risk."
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4))

ate_vals = []
ci_lows = []
ci_highs = []
labels = []
for label, col in treatments.items():
    conf = [c for c in confounders if c != col]
    result = ipw_ate(df_causal, col, "stroke", conf, n_bootstrap=500)
    ate_vals.append(result["ATE"] * 100)
    ci_lows.append(result["CI_low"] * 100)
    ci_highs.append(result["CI_high"] * 100)
    labels.append(label)

y_pos = range(len(labels))
xerr_low = [a - l for a, l in zip(ate_vals, ci_lows)]
xerr_high = [h - a for a, h in zip(ate_vals, ci_highs)]

ax.barh(labels, ate_vals, color=["#E64B35", "#4DBBD5", "#00A087"],
        xerr=[xerr_low, xerr_high], capsize=5, edgecolor="white")
ax.axvline(0, color="black", linewidth=0.5)
ax.set_xlabel("Average Treatment Effect (percentage points)")
ax.set_title("Causal Effect on Stroke Risk (IPW Estimation)")
fig.tight_layout()
savefig(fig, "14_causal_ate.png")
plt.show()
```

**Causal inference assumptions and limitations:**

- **No unmeasured confounding**: We can only adjust for observed variables. Unmeasured
  confounders (genetics, diet, physical activity) may bias estimates.
- **Positivity**: Some covariate strata may have very few treated/untreated, making
  weights unstable. We trim propensity scores to [0.01, 0.99].
- This is observational data — results should be interpreted as **suggestive of causal
  relationships**, not definitive proof.

---

## 6. Analysis IV — Unified Feature Importance Ranking

We synthesise insights from all three methods into a single ranking by combining:

1. **SHAP mean absolute values** (supervised, non-linear)
2. **Logistic regression absolute coefficients** (supervised, linear)
3. **Cluster-stroke association** (unsupervised — how well each feature separates
   high-risk from low-risk clusters)

Each is rank-normalised and averaged.

```{python}
#| label: fig-unified
#| fig-cap: "Unified feature importance ranking combining supervised (SHAP, logistic regression) and unsupervised (cluster separation) evidence. Age and glucose level emerge as the most robust risk factors across all methods."
# SHAP importance
shap_importance = np.abs(shap_values[:, :, 1]).mean(axis=0)
shap_rank = pd.Series(shap_importance, index=feature_names).rank(ascending=False)

# LR importance
lr_importance = np.abs(lr_final.coef_[0])
lr_rank = pd.Series(lr_importance, index=feature_names).rank(ascending=False)

# Cluster-based: correlation of each feature with cluster stroke rate
cluster_stroke = df.groupby("cluster")["stroke"].mean()
feature_cluster_corr = []
for i, fname in enumerate(feature_names):
    cluster_means = pd.Series(X[:, i]).groupby(df["cluster"].values).mean()
    corr_val = abs(cluster_means.corr(cluster_stroke))
    feature_cluster_corr.append(corr_val if not np.isnan(corr_val) else 0)
cluster_rank = pd.Series(feature_cluster_corr, index=feature_names).rank(ascending=False)

# Combine ranks (lower = more important)
combined = pd.DataFrame({
    "SHAP Rank": shap_rank,
    "LR Rank": lr_rank,
    "Cluster Rank": cluster_rank,
})
combined["Mean Rank"] = combined.mean(axis=1)
combined = combined.sort_values("Mean Rank")

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 7))
bar_data = combined["Mean Rank"]
colors = ["#E64B35" if r <= 5 else "#4DBBD5" for r in bar_data.values]
ax.barh(bar_data.index, bar_data.max() - bar_data.values + 1, color=colors)
ax.set_xlabel("Importance Score (higher = more important)")
ax.set_title("Unified Feature Importance — All Methods Combined")
fig.tight_layout()
savefig(fig, "15_unified_importance.png")
plt.show()

combined.head(10)
```

---

## 7. Summary and Conclusions

### Converging Evidence Across All Four Approaches

| Rank | Feature | Supervised | Unsupervised | Causal |
|------|---------|-----------|--------------|--------|
| 1 | **Age** | Top SHAP + LR coef | Separates high/low-risk clusters | Confound-adjusted in all models |
| 2 | **Avg glucose level** | Top SHAP + LR coef | High-risk cluster = high glucose | — |
| 3 | **Hypertension** | Strong positive coef | Enriched in high-risk cluster | Significant ATE |
| 4 | **Heart disease** | Strong positive coef | Enriched in high-risk cluster | Significant ATE |
| 5 | **Smoking (ever)** | Moderate SHAP | Moderate enrichment | Positive ATE |

### Uncertainty Quantification

```{python}
#| label: final-uncertainty
print("Cross-validation performance (5-fold, cost-sensitive, no resampling):\n")
for name in ["Logistic Regression", "Random Forest"]:
    model = models[name]
    roc_scores = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=cv, scoring="roc_auc")
    pr_scores = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=cv, scoring="average_precision")
    print(f"{name}:")
    print(f"  ROC-AUC = {roc_scores.mean():.4f} ± {roc_scores.std():.4f}")
    print(f"  PR-AUC  = {pr_scores.mean():.4f} ± {pr_scores.std():.4f}")
```

### Methodological Notes

**Why not SMOTE?** We deliberately avoid synthetic oversampling (SMOTE) because:
(1) it fabricates data points that may be physiologically implausible,
(2) it inflates evaluation metrics without improving real-world performance,
and (3) modern cost-sensitive learners handle severe imbalance effectively
without data augmentation
([Train in Data, 2025](https://www.blog.trainindata.com/should-you-use-imbalanced-learn-in-2025/);
[imbalanced-learn pitfalls](https://imbalanced-learn.org/stable/common_pitfalls.html)).

**Threshold tuning** on the precision-recall curve provides a principled way to
select an operating point that reflects the clinical cost asymmetry (missing a
stroke is far worse than a false alarm).

### Limitations

- **Observational data**: associations do not prove causation. The IPW estimates are
  suggestive but rely on the no-unmeasured-confounding assumption.
- **Class imbalance**: despite cost-sensitive learning, the 1.8% positive rate
  inherently limits precision. All models favour recall over precision.
- **Missing data**: 30.6% of smoking status is unknown (children), limiting
  precision of smoking effect estimates. Age-stratified BMI imputation introduces
  minor noise.
- **Generalisability**: the dataset's demographic composition may not represent
  all populations.
